assets.title: Tier Zero Objects Lack Kerberos Delegation Protection
assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • ATT&CK TA0004: Privilege Escalation
  • ATT&CK T1078: Valid Accounts
  • ATT&CK T1078.002: Domain Accounts

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • Sean Metcalf: Active Directory Security Risk #101: Kerberos Unconstrained Delegation (or How Compromise of a Single Server Can Compromise the Domain)
  • Will Schroeder: S4U2Pwnage
  • GuidePoint Security: Delegating Like a Boss

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • Protected Users Security Group
  • User Account Control
  • Kerberos Delegation Flows
  • Understanding Kerberos Double Hop

Other Reference Documentation

  • Mike Pilkington: Protecting Privileged Domain Accounts: Safeguarding Access Tokens
  • Trimarc Security: Security Microsoft Entra ID Connect (For MSOL and Entra ID Connect service accounts)
  • PowerPUG: A tiny tool to help AD admins tame the Protected Users group
assets.long_description:
Several attacks that rely on abusing Kerberos delegation configurations require the victim user to be neither marked as "Account is sensitive and cannot be delegated" or a member of the PROTECTED USERS security group.

Technical Background

Kerberos delegation is the act of a principal (Service A) impersonating another security principal (User or Computer) to gain access to a 3rd principal (Service B). A common example is a user connecting to an IIS Server that queries a SQL server on behalf of the user requesting data from the web server. Kerberos delegation can be unconstrained or constrained. Attackers may abuse both flavors of delegation to impersonate any users or computers in the domain and escalate their privileges.

During the delegation process, any security principal that is not otherwise protected from delegation may be impersonated by the service trusted to perform delegation. Security principals marked “Account is sensitive and cannot be delegated” or added to the PROTECTED USERS group in AD are effectively “immune” from Kerberos delegation attacks. Microsoft considers protecting privileged accounts from delegation to be the security boundary around Kerberos delegation, rather than whether Kerberos delegation is currently configured or the environment.
assets.long_remediation:
There are two options to remediate these issues:

  • Option A: Add Tier Zero Users to the PROTECTED USERS security group (preferred for non-service accounts)
  • Option B: Mark Users as "Sensitive and Cannot be Delegated"

The PROTECTED USERS group is the preferred remediation option as it protects any member security principal, except the RID 500 Administrators account (Reference 1, Reference 2), from Kerberos delegation while also offering additional protections, including disabling NTLM authentication, credential caching, weak Kerberos encryption, and shorter Kerberos TGT lifetime.

There are, unfortunately, some scenarios where membership in the PROTECTED USERS group is not an option. Microsoft recommends not adding service accounts configured as an AD user or computer object to PROTECTED USERS. Services configured in this manner will fail upon expiration of the Kerberos ticket. Windows services configured as a group-managed service account (gMSA) may function properly as a member of the PROTECTED USERS group, although this is not a Microsoft-recommended practice.

There are some administrative tasks that are not possible when a security principal is a member of PROTECTED USERS. Any such account should also be marked “Sensitive and cannot be delegated” and made a member of PROTECTED USERS, except for when performing those rare tasks. Other administrative tasks may become inconvenient when sensitive users are added to PROTECTED USERS. This is generally an indicator that the previous procedures for those tasks were insecure.

There are no downsides to a security principal being marked “Sensitive and cannot be delegated” AND a member of PROTECTED USERS. The RID500 Administrator account should be configured as such to receive delegation protections and the additional PROTECTED USERS group protections.

In many AD environments, it is often more straightforward to enable delegation protection by marking users “Sensitive and cannot be delegated” first, as this requires less testing and process modification. Then, as auditing and testing is performed to prepare accounts for PROTECTED USERS membership, the “Sensitive and cannot be delegated” flag can remain with no ill effect.

Option A: Add Tier Zero Users to the PROTECTED USERS Security Group

  1. Open AD Users and Computers (ADUC) with a Tier Zero account (for example, a member of the DOMAIN ADMINS group)

  2. Locate the PROTECTED USERS security group (the default location of this group is directly under the default "Users" container)

  3. Right-click on the PROTECTED USERS group and click on "Properties"

  4. Click on the "Members" tab in the "Properties" window

  5. For each Tier Zero user, click "Add...", find the Tier Zero user, and then click "Ok"

  6. After all Tier Zero users have been added to the group, click "Apply" and "Ok"

Option B: Mark Users as "Sensitive and Cannot be Delegated"

For each user that needs to be marked as "Sensitive and Cannot be Delegated", take the following actions:

  1. Use a Tier Zero account (e.g., a member of the DOMAIN ADMINS group) to log into ADUC

  2. Locate the user by either using the search function or navigating to the user in the organizational unit (OU) tree

  3. Right-click the user and click on "Properties"

  4. Click on the "Account" tab in the "User Properties" window

  5. In the "Account options" list, scroll down to the "Account is Sensitive and Cannot be Delegated" item

  6. Click the checkbox next to the "Account is sensitive and cannot be delegated" list item, then click "Ok"

assets.title: Kerberoastable User Accounts
assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • ATT&CK T1558: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
  • ATT&CK T1558.003: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: Kerberoasting

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • Sean Metcalf: Cracking Kerberos TGS Tickets Using Kerberoast – Exploiting Kerberos to Compromise the Active Directory Domain
  • Sean Metcalf and Will Schroeder: DerbyCon 2016 - 111 Attacking EvilCorp Anatomy of a Corporate Hack

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • Kerberos Explained
assets.long_description:
Services hosted on Windows systems that support Kerberos authentication should use computer accounts as the service principal. If the application instead uses a user account for Kerberos authentication, the user account should have a very strong and complex password comprised of at least 32 characters or be enrolled in a privileged access management (PAM) solution that manages the password for the account.

Technical Background

During the Kerberos authentication process, principals may request a ticket-granting service (TGS) ticket for a service from a domain controller (DC). The validity of that TGS ticket is validated by the service principal due to the TGS ticket being signed and encrypted with the NT hash of the service principal's password.

This process makes the password of the service principal susceptible to offline brute-force attacks, as any domain authenticated user or computer can request the TGS ticket. An attacker with possession of this TGS ticket could perform an offline brute-force attack which, if successful, recovers the plaintext password of the service principal. This then allows the attacker to impersonate the service principal on the domain.
assets.long_remediation:
Note: The risk associated with each account in this Attack Path wholly depends on the strength of the account's password. It is likely that any environment will always have user accounts that the Kerberoast attack can target. The goal is not necessarily to reduce the number of users listed above to zero but to ensure all privileged accounts listed have very strong passwords.

AD user objects become susceptible to the Kerberoast account when they have any value in their "serviceprincipalnames" attribute and the success of the Kerberoast attack is wholly dependent on the strength of the affected account's password. The impact of a successful Kerberoast attack is revealed by how privileged the affected user is, as the attacker will then gain that level of privilege in the domain.

Two options exist for remediation for each affected account:

  • Clear the "serviceprincipalname" attribute on the user object
  • Increase strength of the user's password

Option A: Clear the "serviceprincipalname" Attribute on the User Object

If possible, remove all entries in the serviceprincipalnames attribute on the user object. This will make the user no longer vulnerable to the Kerberoast attack and is the best permanent remediation for each user. To clear this property on the user object, take the following steps:

  1. Log onto a Tier Zero computer as a Tier Zero user and open AD Users and Computers (ADUC)

  2. Locate the user in the organizational unit (OU) tree, right-click the user, and then click on "Properties"

  3. Click on the "Attribute Editor" tab, scroll down to the serviceprincipalname attribute , highlight it, and click "Edit"

  4. For each entry on this attribute, highlight the entry and click "Remove"

  5. Once all entries are removed, click "Ok" and repeat all steps for each affected user

If the "serviceprincipalname" attribute on the user object can not be fully cleared out, then refer to Option B

Option B: Enforce a Very Strong, Complex Password Comprised of at Least 32 characters or Enroll the Account in a PAM That Manages the Password

If a password vaulting solution is in place, log into the solution, locate the account, and view the cleartext password for the account. If the password is particularly weak, it is possible that the Kerberoast attack has already compromised the account.

If a password vaulting solution does not manage the user's password and no staff members know the cleartext password, assume that the password is weak and easily guessable.

Therefore, it is critical to set up a parameter for that account in the password vault so that the user's password is sufficiently strong.

The password is considered sufficiently strong and not susceptible to the Kerberoast attack if it is:

  • At least 32 characters in length
  • Comprised of uppercase, lowercase, numeral, and special characters
assets.short_description:
Services hosted on Windows systems that support Kerberos authentication should use computer accounts as the service principal. During the Kerberos authentication process, principals may request a ticket-granting service (TGS) ticket for a service from a domain controller. The validity of that TGS ticket is validated by the service principal due to the TGS ticket being signed and encrypted with the NT hash of the service principal's password.

This process makes the service principal's password susceptible to offline brute force attacks, as the TGS ticket may be requested by any domain authenticated user or computer. Then, an attacker with possession of this TGS ticket may perform an offline brute force attack which, if successful, recovers the plaintext password of the service principal.
assets.short_remediation:
• Option A: Clear the "serviceprincipalname" attribute on the user object and reconfigure the service to use a computer account as its service principal

  • Option B: Enforce a very strong, complex password comprised of at least 32 characters or enroll the account in a privileged access management (PAM) solution that manages the password

assets.title: AS-REP Roastable User Accounts
assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • ATT&CK T1558: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets
  • ATT&CK T1558.004: Steal or Forge Kerberos Tickets: AS-REP Roasting

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • Will Schroeder: Roasting AS-REPs

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • Kerberos Pre-Authentication: Why It Should Not Be Disabled
  • Kerberos Explained
assets.long_description:
Kerberos pre-authentication is the first step of Kerberos authentication and is intended to help prevent password brute-force attacks. Organizations can configure accounts to remove this requirement, generally when an account authenticates to an application that does not support Kerberos pre-authentication.

Technical Background

During the Kerberos authentication process, when a user initiates a ticket-granting ticket (TGT) request, a timestamp encrypted with the user's key/password is provided to the Key Distribution Center (KDC). The KDC decrypts this timestamp to validate the legitimacy of the "as requestor" (AS-REQ) request. When an account is marked with the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag, this step is ignored.

This makes the account susceptible to offline brute-force attacks, as attackers can request AS-REQs for any users with this flag set and receive encrypted credential material. An attacker in possession of this credential material could perform an offline brute-force attack which, if successful, recovers the plaintext password of the user account. This then allows the user to impersonate the user account on the domain.
assets.long_remediation:
Note: The risk associated with each account listed in the chart above is wholly dependent on the strength of the account's password. The goal is not necessarily to reduce the number of users listed above to zero, but to ensure every listed privileged account has a very strong password.

User objects in AD become susceptible to the AS-REP Roast attack when they have the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag set and the success of the attack wholly depends on the strength of the affected account's password. The impact of a successful AS-REP Roast attack is revealed by how privileged the affected user is, as the attacker will then gain that level of privilege in the domain.

Two options exist for remediation for each affected account: * Remove the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag from the user object * Increase the strength of the user's password

Option A: Clear the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" Attribute on the User Object

If possible, remove the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag on the user object. This will make the user no longer vulnerable to the AS-REP Roasting attack and is the best permanent remediation for each user. To clear this flag on the user object, take the following steps: 1. Log onto a Tier Zero computer as a Tier Zero user and open AD Users and Computers (ADUC) 2. Locate the user in the organizational unit (OU) tree 3. Right-click the user, then click "Properties" 4. Click the "Account" tab 5. In the "Account options" section, scroll down to the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag 6. Uncheck the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag and then click "Apply"

If the account requires this flag, then refer to Option B.

Option B: Enforce a very strong and complex password comprised of at least 32 characters or enroll the account in a privileged access management (PAM) solution that manages the password

If a password vaulting solution is in place, log into the solution, locate the account, and view the cleartext password for the account. If the password is particularly weak, it is possible that an attacker has already used the Kerberoast attack to compromise the account.

If a password vaulting solution does not manage the user's password, nor do any staff members know the cleartext password, assume that the password is weak and easily guessable.

Therefore, it is critical to set up a parameter for that account in the password vault so that the user's password is sufficiently strong.

The password is considered sufficiently strong and not susceptible to the Kerberoast attack if it is:

  • At least 32 characters in length
  • Comprised of uppercase, lowercase, numeral, and special characters
assets.short_description:
Kerberos pre-authentication is the first step of Kerberos authentication and is intended to help prevent password brute-force attacks. Organizations can configure accounts to remove this requirement, generally when an account authenticates to an application that does not support Kerberos pre-authentication.

Users marked as "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" allow attackers to request the account's AS-REP request from any domain authenticated user or computers. Then, an attacker with possession of this AS-REP request may perform an offline brute-force attack which, if successful, recovers the plaintext password of the user account.
assets.short_remediation:
• Option A: Remove the "Do not require Kerberos preauthentication" flag from the User Object
  • Option B: Enforce a very strong, complex password comprised of at least 32 characters or enroll the account in a privileged access management (PAM) solution that manages the password

assets.title: Non-Tier Zero Principals With ADCS ESC3 Privileges
assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • ATT&CK T1649: Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • BloodHound Docs: ADCSESC3
  • Will Schroeder and Lee Christensen: Certified Pre-Owned
  • Will Schroeder and Lee Christensen: Certify
  • Oliver Lyak: Certipy

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • [MS-CERSOD]: Enroll on Behalf of Request and Renewal
assets.long_description:
Only Tier Zero principals should be allowed to enroll certificates on behalf of other Tier Zero principals in the AD forest.

Technical Background

The principal has permission to enroll on a certificate template with the Certificate Request Agent enhanced key usage (EKU) (OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.1), allowing them to obtain an enrollment agent certificate. The principal also has permission to enroll on a certificate template that permits enrollment by enrollment agents and can be used for authentication. Additionally, they also have enrollment permissions for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest along with the certificate chain up to the root CA certificate. This setup lets the principal enroll certificates for any AD forest user or computer, enabling authentication and impersonation of any AD forest user or computer without their credentials, unless enrollment agent restrictions on the enterprise CA protect the target user or computer.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC3 edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC3 edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click on "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

BloodHound does not consider the following aspects of enrollment agent restrictions: - Deny rules - Target principals of rules This finding may not be abusable if such restrictions are in place to prevent the non-Tier Zero principal of the finding from enrolling certificates on behalf of every Tier Zero principal (including computers). You can follow the steps provided in the Implement Enrollment Agent Restrictions section to verify the enrollment agent restrictions.

We advise gaining a clear understanding of the intended use of the certificate templates to determine the most suitable remediation approach. This can be achieved through an evaluation of existing certificates and authentication logs, as outlined in the Certified Pre-Owned ADCS whitepaper sections:

  • Monitor User/Machine Certificate Enrollments - DETECT1
  • Monitor Certificate Authentication Events - DETECT2

Collaborate with the individual responsible for ADCS within the organization to address the following questions pertaining to the identified certificate templates. This process will help in considering the appropriate checks and remedial actions described below:

  1. Is the certificate template in use?
    Check: Latest issued certificates and expiration dates Remediation: Unpublish (Disable) Certificate Template

  2. Which principals are enrolling in the templates?
    Check: Requester principals of issued certificates Remediation: Remove Enroll Permission (Restrict to Tier Zero)

  3. Which principals are enrolling as enrollment agents?
    Check: Request 'CallerName' of issued certificates Remediation: Implement enrollment agent restrictions

  4. Does the certificate template need to allow for authentication?
    Check: Login events using certificates created with the certificate template Remediation: Remove Extended Key Usage (EKU) That Enables Authentication

  5. Could future certificate requests wait for a manual approval?
    Check: If it is feasible that the certificate request has to wait for a Tier Zero principal to manually approve the request Remediation: Enable Manager Approval

Unpublish (Disable) Certificate Template

For every Enterprise certificate authority (CA) in the finding:

  1. Log in on the Enterprise CA host with a principal that has the right to unpublish certificate templates from the Enterprise CA (only ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS have this privilege by default in the forest root domain)

  2. Open "Certification Authority" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running certsrv.msc:

  3. Browse to the certificate template, right-click, and click "Delete" (this will only unpublish the certificate template from the given CA, NOT delete the certificate template):

  4. Click "Yes" in the prompt to confirm that you want to disable (unpublish) the certificate template

Remove Enroll Permission (Restrict to Tier Zero)

  1. Log in on an Enterprise CA host with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template permissions (only ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS have this privilege by default in the forest root domain)

  2. Open "Certification Authority" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running certsrv.msc:

  3. Right-click on "Certificate Templates" and click "Manage" to open the Certificate Templates Console:

  4. Right-click on the certificate template for the finding and click "Properties":

  5. Under "Security", remove the "Full Control" and "Enroll" permissions for the non-Tier Zero principals, and click "OK":

Removing "Full Control" remediates "GenericAll" BloodHound edges against the certificate template and removing "Enroll" remediates "AllExtendedRights" and "Enroll" edges.

Implement Enrollment Agent Restrictions

  1. Log in on an Enterprise CA host with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template permissions (only ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS have this privilege by default in the forest root domain)

  2. Open "Certification Authority" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running certsrv.msc:

  3. Right-click on the CA, click "Properties", and click "Enrollment Agents":

  4. Add or modify the existing rules to protect Tier Zero principals from non-Tier Zero enrollment agents

SpecterOps recommends implementing a rule to only allow the enrollment agents to enroll on behalf of the principals that they are intended to enroll on behalf of and restrict the rule to the intended certificate templates and deny other principals in the "Permissions" section, most importantly Tier Zero principals (including computers). This will allow for the enrollment agents to be considered as non-Tier Zero. If Tier Zero principals need to be the allowed as target (under "Permissions"), then the enrollment agents for that rule would have to be considered Tier Zero. Note that "Deny" rules overrule "Allow" rules.

Remove EKU That Enables Authentication

We will modify the EKUs directly on certificate template AD object, as it is not possible to edit EKUs of schema version 1 certificate templates in the Certificate Templates Console.

The effective EKUs of a certificate template are stored either in "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" or "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" depending of the schema version (i.e., "msPKI-Template-Schema-Version") of the certificate template. "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" holds the effective EKUs if the schema version is higher that 1 or if "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" is empty (null). Else, "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" holds the effective EKUs. We recommend to have the same content in both attributes to avoid any mistakes.

  1. Log in on a domain controller (DC) of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS have this privilege by default in the forest root domain)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click on "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" Naming Context and click on "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" and click "Edit":
    (image: 5-0-6-adsi-mspki-cap.png)

  7. Select and click "Remove" for any EKU matching the following and click "OK":

    - 2.5.29.37.0 (Any Purpose)

- 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.2 (Client Authentication)

- 1.3.6.1.5.2.3.4 (PKINIT Client Authentication)

- 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.2 (Smart Card Logon)

  8. Repeat the sixth and seventh steps for the "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" attribute and click "OK" to apply the changes

  9. If there are no EKUs left in "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" or "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" after the removal of the authentication EKUs, then this remediation will become ineffective as no EKUs is interpreted as any EKU; if that is the case, one of the other remediation options must be followed

Enable Manager Approval

If the certificate template is of schema version 2 or above, then it is possible to require manager approval by enabling "CA Certificate Manager Approval" in the Certificate Templates Console on a CA server:

The following instructions apply to certificate templates of any schema version.

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS have this privilege by default in the forest root domain)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag" and click "Edit":

  7. Add the number 2 to the existing value and click "OK"

Note: For example, if the current value is 8, then 8 plus 2 is equal to 10:

  1. Click "OK" to apply the changes

  2. Certificate requests of this certificate template will appear under "Pending Requests" in "Certification Authority" on the CA server from where the requests can be approved or denied:

  3. Consider carefully if the principal in "Requester Name" is allowed to take the identity of the principal specified in the SAN attribute of the request approving the request:

This allows an attacker to enroll a certificate on behalf of another user and use it to authenticate as this user. The target can be any user in the AD forest unless enrollment agent restrictions protect them.
assets.short_remediation: Remediating this finding can be approached in several ways, such as unpublishing the affected template(s), restricting enrollment permissions, modifying enrollement agent settings, or prohibiting the use of the certificate for authentication.

assets.long_description:
Only Tier Zero principals should be allowed to enroll certificates as other Tier Zero principals in the AD forest.

Technical Background

The principal has control over a victim principal with permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates, configured to enable certificate authentication and require the "userPrincipalName" (UPN) of the enrollee included in the "subjectAlternativeName" (SAN). The victim also has enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. There is an affected domain controller (DC) configured to allow UPN certificate mapping. This setup lets the principal impersonate any AD forest computer or any user where UPN does not match their "sAMAccountName" without knowing their credentials.

The attacker principal can abuse their control over the victim principal to modify the victim's UPN to match the "sAMAccountName" of a targeted principal followed by "@CORP.LOCAL".

Example: If the targeted principal is the Administrator user of domain "CORP.LOCAL", then the victim's UPN will be populated with "ADMINISTRATOR@CORP.LOCAL".

The attacker principal will then abuse their control over the victim principal to obtain the credentials of the victim principal, or a session as the victim principal, and enroll a certificate as the victim in one of the affected certificate templates. The UPN of the victim (i.e., "ADMINISTRATOR@CORP.LOCAL") will be included in the issued certificate under the SAN. Next, the attacker principal will again set the UPN of the victim, this time to an arbitrary string (e.g., the original value). The issued certificate can now be used for authentication against an affected DC.

The UPN certificate mapping configuration on the DC make the DC use the SAN value to map the certificate to a principal when performing Schannel authentication. The DC will attempt to find a principal with a UPN matching the SAN value (i.e., "Administrator@CORP.LOCAL"); however, since the victim's UPN changed after the enrollment, there will be no principals with this UPN. The DC will then attempt to find a principal with a "sAMAccountName" matching the SAN value and find the targeted user. In case the target is a computer, the DC will find it as well as the DC will attempt "sAMAccountName" matching with a "$" at the end of the SAN value as a last resort. At last, the DC will authenticate the attacker as the targeted principal.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC10a edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC10a edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

The core of the issue are the affected DCs which have the weak userPrincipalName (UPN) certificate mapping for Schannel authentication enabled. The domain controllers (DCs) should be reconfigured to only allow strong certificate mapping to remediate the finding.

The certificate mapping is configured with the registry value CertificateMappingMethods under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\Schannel on the DCs. It is bitwise AND value with the possible flags:

  • 0x01 = Subject/Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)
  • 0x02 = Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)
  • 0x04 = UPN certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)
  • 0x08 = S4U2Self certificate mapping (strong)
  • 0x10 = S4U2Self explicit certificate mapping (strong)

Identify DCs With "Explore" Certificate Mapping Enabled

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound Explore tab to list DCs configured with UPN certificate mapping enabled: MATCH p = (dc:Computer)-[:DCFor]->(:Domain) WHERE dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw >= 0 AND dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw IN [4, 5, 6, 7, 12, 13, 14, 15, 20, 21, 22, 23, 28, 29, 30, 31] RETURN p There may be additional DCs configured with UPN certificate mapping enabled if SharpHound has not performed a successful registry collection on all DCs (requires admin rights). To list all DCs where the Schannel certificate mapping configuration has not been collected, run the cypher query: MATCH p = (dc:Computer)-[:DCFor]->(:Domain) WHERE dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw IS NULL OR dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw = "-1" RETURN p

Run this PowerShell snippet to check the current value flags enabled the certificate mapping configuration on a given DC: ``` $curValue = (Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\Schannel" -Name "CertificateMappingMethods").CertificateMappingMethods

$flagTable = @{ 0x0001 = "Subject/Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)" 0x0002 = "Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)" 0x0004 = "UPN certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)" 0x0008 = "S4U2Self certificate mapping (strong)" 0x0010 = "S4U2Self explicit certificate mapping (strong)" }

foreach ($flag in $flagTable.Keys) { if ($curValue -band $flag) { "0x$("{0:X4}" -f $flag) $($flagTable[$flag])" } } ```

Identify Certificates Incompatible With Strong Certificate Mapping

A DC will generate an event in the Security log with Windows Event ID (EID) 4624 with the "Logon Process" field set to "Schannel" when the DC authenticates a principal over Schannel. Whether strong or weak certificate mapping was used is revealed in the "Authentication Package" field:

  • Strong certificate mapping = Authentication Package: Kerberos
  • Weak certificate mapping = Authentication Package: Microsoft Unified Security Protocol Provider

Defenders can use the network address and principal names in this event to track down Schannel-based server applications where new certificates must be put in place before strong certificate mapping is enforced, to avoid breaking authentication for the applications.

Ensure the DCs generating these events have strong certificate mapping enabled and not only weak certificate mapping. If strong certificate mapping is not enabled, the DC has no other option than to use weak certificate mapping, even if a certificate complies with strong certificate mapping.

Enforce Strong Certificate Mapping for Schannel Authentication

  1. Log in on the DC as a member of the ADMINISTRATORS group
  2. Open PowerShell as an administrator
  3. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to enforce strong certificate mapping: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\Schannel" -Name "CertificateMappingMethods" -Value 24

Restart of the DC is not required.

Windows event ID (EID) 41 in the System|Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log on DCs reveals failed attempts to authenticate with certificates not compliant with the strong certificate mapping:

Defenders can use these events to catch whether any system breaks under the enforcement of strong certificate mapping.

assets.long_description: The principal has permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates that allow for authentication. They also have enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. AD trusts this enterprise CA for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. The enterprise CA is configured with the EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 flag, which allows enrollees to specify a "subjectAlternateName" (SAN) that identifies another principal during certificate enrollment of any published certificate template. This setup allows an attacker principal to obtain a malicious certificate as any AD forest user or computer and use it for authentication and impersonation without knowning their credentials.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC6a edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC6a edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

We advise gaining a clear understanding of the current use of the "subjectAlternateName" (SAN) functionality enabled by the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag before removing it from the Enterprise certificate authorities (CAs).

When "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" is utilized, the certificate request incorporates the SAN name as a certificate attribute. In contrast, using a certificate template with the "ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT" flag places the SAN name in a certificate extension. To identify certificate requests that utilize "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2", one can search for requests with a SAN name as an attribute. This can be achieved using PSPKIAudit's Get-CertRequest function as a CA Administrator: ``` PS C:> Get-CertRequest -CAName dumpster-DC01-CA -HasSAN | ? {$_.SubjectAltNamesAttrib -ne ""}

CA : DC01.dumpster.fire\dumpster-DC01-CA RequestID : 180 RequesterName : DUMPSTER\jd RequesterMachineName : DC01.dumpster.fire RequesterProcessName : Certify.exe SubjectAltNamesExtension : Roshi SubjectAltNamesAttrib : Roshi SerialNumber : 7b000000b4822ab66661d867a10000000000b4 CertificateTemplate : ClientAuth2 (1.3.6.1.4.1.311.21.8.4571196.1884641.3293620.10686285.12068043.134.3767878.1854842) RequestDate : 12/13/2023 7:23:13 PM StartDate : 12/13/2023 7:13:13 PM EndDate : 12/12/2024 7:13:13 PM ```

If no such certificate requests are found, it is safe to remove the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag on the enterprise CA server.

Certificate requests with expired end date and certificate requests created during testing can be disregarded. For the remaining certificate requests, it is advisable to analyze the certificate templates used, to determine how the certificate templates can be reconfigured to meet business needs without relying on the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag on the enterprise CA server.

Since a certificate template can be published to multiple enterprise CAs, it is recommended to assess the usage of a certificate template on all enterprise CAs where templates are published, not just those with the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag. Use the following BloodHound cypher query to determine the enterprise CAs a template is published to: MATCH p = (ct:CertTemplate)-[:PublishedTo]->(:EnterpriseCA) WHERE ct.name = "MYTEMPLATE@DOMAIN.LOCAL" RETURN p

The optimal solution for certificate templates depends on their usage: - If a template is used with and without a specified SAN, consider creating two distinct certificate templates to isolate the issue where the SAN is specified - If the requester and the principal specified in the SAN are the same, the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" may not be necessary as no impersonation is required - For templates requiring impersonation, explore replacing the template with an enrollment agent setup (see References) - Consider restricting enrollment rights on the certificate template to permit only Tier Zero principals to enroll; in such cases, enabling the "ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT" flag on the template allows for supplying a SAN, eliminating the need for the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag on the enterprise CA - However, it is essential to reconfigure the enrollment process to leverage a certification extension instead of a certificate attribute for the provided SAN

Remove "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" Flag

  1. Log in on the enterprise CA server as CA Administrator or member of the ADMINISTRATORS group on the host
  2. Open CMD as Administrator

  3. To see if "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECALTNAME2" is enabled on the CA, run the following command: certutil -getreg policy\EditFlags If "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" is included in the output, it is turned on.

  4. To disable the setting, run the following command: certutil -setreg policy\EditFlags -EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2

  5. Then restart the CA service: net stop certsvc && net start certsvc
assets.short_description: The principal can perform the AD Certificate Services (ADCS) ESC6 Scenario A attack against the target domain. This attack allows the principal to impersonate any other principal in the forest.
assets.short_remediation: Remove the EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 flag from enterprise certificate authorities (CAs).

assets.long_description:
Only Tier Zero principals should be allowed to enroll certificates as other Tier Zero principals in the AD forest.

Technical Background

The principal has control over a victim principal with permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates, configured to: 1) enable certificate authentication, 2) require the "userPrincipalName" (UPN) of the enrollee included in the "subjectAlternativeName" (SAN), and 3) do not have the security extension enabled. The victim also has enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. There is an affected domain controller (DC) configured to allow weak certificate binding enforcement. This setup lets the principal impersonate any AD forest principal (user or computer) without their credentials.

The attacker principal can abuse their control over the victim principal to modify the victim's UPN to match the "sAMAccountName" of a targeted principal.

Example: If the targeted principal is the ADMINISTRATOR@CORP.LOCAL user, the victim's UPN will be populated with "ADMINISTRATOR" (without the "@CORP.LOCAL" ending).

The attacker principal will then abuse their control over the victim principal to obtain the credentials of the victim principal, or a session as the victim principal, and enroll a certificate as the victim in one of the affected certificate templates. The UPN of the victim (i.e., "Administrator") will appear in the issued certificate under the SAN. As the certificate template does not have the security extension, it will NOT include the security ID (SID) of the victim user in the issued certificate.

Next, the attacker principal will again set the UPN of the victim, this time to an arbitrary string (e.g., the original value). The issued certificate can now be used for authentication against an affected DC. The weak certificate binding configuration on the DC will make the DC accept that the SID of the victim user is not present in the issued certificate when performing Kerberos authentication, and it will use the SAN value to map the certificate to a principal. The DC will attempt to find a principal with a UPN matching the SAN value (i.e., "Administrator") but as the victim's UPN has been changed after the enrollment, there will be no principals with this UPN. The DC will then attempt to find a principal with a "sAMAccountName" that matches the SAN value and find the targeted user.

At last, the DC issues a Kerberos ticket-granting ticket (TGT) as the targeted user to the attacker, which means the attacker now has a session as the targeted user. In case the target is a computer, the DC will find it as well as the DC will attempt to match the "sAMAccountName" with a "$" at the end of the SAN value as a last resort.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC9a edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC9a edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

The best way of remediating the issue is to perform the follow two steps in the specified order:

  1. Remove the "NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION" flag from certificate templates

  2. Enforce strong certificate binding on all domain controllers (DCs)

We advise gaining a clear understanding of the current usage in the organization of the affected certificate templates with the "NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION" flag to determine if enforcing strong certificate binding could break current workflows, and fix those before performing the remediation.

As alternative remediations, you can either harden the affected certificate templates to only allow Tier Zero principals to enroll or enable manager approval. The alternative solutions will likely be temporary, as Microsoft have stated that they will enforce strong certificate binding with a security update in the future (see Reference: KB5014754 - Certificate-Based Authentication Changes on Windows Domain Controllers).

Identify CertTemplates With no Security Extension

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound "Explore" tab to list "CertTemplates" with no security extension:

MATCH (ct:CertTemplate) WHERE ct.nosecurityextension = true RETURN ct

Identify DCs With Weak Certificate Binding Enforcement

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound "Explore" tab to list DCs configured with weak certificate binding:

MATCH (n:Computer) WHERE n.isdc = true AND n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw < 2 RETURN n

There may be additional DCs configured with weak certificate binding if SharpHound has not performed a successful registry collection on all DCs (requires admin rights). To list all DCs where the certificate binding configuration has not been collected, run:

MATCH (n:Computer) WHERE n.isdc = true AND (n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw IS NULL OR n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw = -1) RETURN n

The DC is configured with weak certificate binding enforcement if the "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement" registry value under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc is set to 0 (Disabled) or 1 (Combability mode).

Identify Certificates Incompatible With Strong Certificate Binding

The Windows May 10, 2022 security update provides audit events that identify certificates that are not compatible with the strong certificate binding. The event IDs (EIDs) are 39-41 under the System\Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log. You should collect and examine those events across DCs to identify any incompatible certificates.

Collaborate with the individuals responsible for AD Certificate Services (ADCS) within the organization to address issues.

Note: The KB article describing the audit events states that the event source is Kdcsvc; however, we have not been able to find the events in this source, but in the "Microsoft-Windows-Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center".

Recommended Remediation: Enforce Strong Certificate Binding

  1. Log in on the DC as a member of the ADMINISTRATORS group

  2. Open PowerShell as an administrator

  3. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to check the current value of the certificate binding enforcement: Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement"
    The value is configured with an integer:
    0: Disabled
    1: Combability mode
    2: Full enforcement mode

  4. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to set strong certificate binding enforcement: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement" -Value 2

  5. Verify that the registry value was updated by checking the value again: Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement"

Restart of the DC is not required.

Windows EID 41 in the System\Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log on DCs will reveal failed attempts to authenticate with certificates not compliant with the strong certificate binding:

Alternative Remediation: Remove No Security Extension Flag

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege by default)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag" and click "Edit":

  7. Extract 524288 from the existing value and click "OK"; for example, if the current value is 524296, then 524296 minus 524288 is equal to 8:

  8. Click "OK" to apply the changes

Alternative Remediation: Enable Manager Approval

While manager approval is enabled on a certificate template, the certificate authority doesn't automatically issue the requested certificate. Instead, issuance occurs only after a CA administrator or CA officer manually approves it.

Manager approval can be activated for certificate templates of any schema version. For certificate templates with schema version 2 or higher, you can enable manager approval specifically by toggling the "CA Certificate Manager Approval" setting within the Certificate Templates Console on the CA server:

The following instructions apply to certificate templates of any schema version.

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege by default)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag" and click "Edit":

  7. Add the number 2 to the existing value and click "OK"; for example, if the current value is 8, then 8 plus 2 is equal to 10:

  8. Click "OK" to apply the changes

  9. Certificate requests of this certificate template will appear under "Pending Requests" in "Certification Authority" on the CA server from where the requests can be approved or denied:  

  10. Consider carefully whether the principal in "Requester Name" is allowed to take the identity of the principal specified in the "subjectAlternateName" (SAN) extension of the request before approving the request:

assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • What is Active Directory Certificate Services?

Other Reference Documentation

  • PSPKIAudit

assets.long_description:
The principal has permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates allowing for authentication. They also have enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. The enterprise CA is configured with the EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 flag, allowing enrollees to specify a "subjectAlternateName" (SAN) identifying another principal during certificate enrollment of any published certificate template.

This setup allows an attacker principal to obtain a malicious certificate as another principal. There is an affected domain controller (DC) configured to allow weak certificate mapping enforcement, which enables the attacker principal to authenticate with the malicious certificate and thereby impersonate any AD forest user or computer without their credentials.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC6b edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC6b edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

We advise gaining a clear understanding of the current use of the SAN functionality enabled by the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag before removing it from the Enterprise certificate authorities (CAs).

When "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" is utilized, the certificate request incorporates the "subjectAlternateName" (SAN) name as a certificate attribute. In contrast, using a certificate template with the "ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT" flag places the SAN name in a certificate extension. To identify certificate requests that utilized "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2", one can search for requests with a SAN name as an attribute. This can be achieved using PSPKIAudit's Get-CertRequest function as a CA Administrator: ``` PS C:> Get-CertRequest -CAName dumpster-DC01-CA -HasSAN | ? {$_.SubjectAltNamesAttrib -ne ""}

CA : DC01.dumpster.fire\dumpster-DC01-CA RequestID : 180 RequesterName : DUMPSTER\jd RequesterMachineName : DC01.dumpster.fire RequesterProcessName : Certify.exe SubjectAltNamesExtension : Roshi SubjectAltNamesAttrib : Roshi SerialNumber : 7b000000b4822ab66661d867a10000000000b4 CertificateTemplate : ClientAuth2 (1.3.6.1.4.1.311.21.8.4571196.1884641.3293620.10686285.12068043.134.3767878.1854842) RequestDate : 12/13/2023 7:23:13 PM StartDate : 12/13/2023 7:13:13 PM EndDate : 12/12/2024 7:13:13 PM ```

If no such certificate requests are found, it is safe to remove the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag on the enterprise CA server.

Certificate requests with expired end date and certificate requests created during testing can be disregarded. For the remaining certificate requests, it is advisable to analyze the certificate templates used, to determine how the certificate templates can be reconfigured to meet business needs without relying on the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag on the enterprise CA server.

Since a certificate template can be published to multiple enterprise CAs, it is recommended to assess the usage of a certificate template on all enterprise CAs where templates are published, not just those with the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag. To determine the enterprise CAs a template is published to, use the following BloodHound cypher query: MATCH p = (ct:CertTemplate)-[:PublishedTo]->(:EnterpriseCA) WHERE ct.name = "MYTEMPLATE@DOMAIN.LOCAL" RETURN p

The optimal solution for certificate templates depends on their usage: - If a template is used with and without a specified SAN, consider creating two distinct certificate templates to isolate the issue where the SAN is specified - If the requester and the principal specified in the SAN are the same, the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" may not be necessary as no impersonation is required - For templates requiring impersonation, explore replacing the template with an enrollment agent setup (see References) - Consider restricting enrollment rights on the certificate template to permit only Tier Zero principals to enroll; in such cases, enabling the "ENROLLEE_SUPPLIES_SUBJECT" flag on the template allows for supplying a SAN, eliminating the need for the "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" flag on the enterprise CA - However, it is essential to reconfigure the enrollment process to leverage a certification extension instead of a certificate attribute for the provided SAN

Remove "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" Flag

  1. Log in on the enterprise CA server as a CA Administrator or member of the ADMINISTRATORS group on the host
  2. Open CMD as an administrator

  3. To see if "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECALTNAME2" is enabled on the CA, run the following command: certutil -getreg policy\EditFlags If "EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2" is included in the output, it is turned on.

  4. To disable the setting, run the following command: certutil -setreg policy\EditFlags -EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2

  5. Then restart the CA service: net stop certsvc && net start certsvc

assets.short_remediation: Remove the EDITF_ATTRIBUTESUBJECTALTNAME2 flag from enterprise certificate authorities (CAs).
