assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • Steal or Forge Authentication Certificates

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • BloodHound Docs: ADCSESC1
  • Misconfigured Certificate Templates - ESC1

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • What is Active Directory Certificate Services?

Other Reference Documentation

  • Certified Pre-Owned - Abusing Active Directory Certificate Services
  • Certipy
  • HIDEANDSEC - ADCS
assets.long_description: The principal has permissions to enroll on one or more certificate templates, allowing them to specify an alternate subject name (SAN) and use the certificate for authentication. They also have enrollment permissions for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest, along with the certificate chain up to the root CA certificate. This setup lets the principal enroll certificates for any AD forest user or computer, enabling authentication and impersonation of any AD forest user or computer without their credentials.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC1 edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC1 edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click on "Explore"

  1. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  2. As the ADCS finding is the effective outcome of the configuration and relationships between several different objects, click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved:

  1. Change the "Layout" to "Sequential" to explore the path from left to right:

  1. The certificate template(s) related to the finding are those to which the principal has enrollment rights:

Note: Certificate templates are "PublishedTo" one or more Enterprise certificate authorities (CA). The CA Name in BloodHound may be different from the hostname of the CA.

  1. Click the "CA" node to view the DNS hostname of the CA

We advise gaining a clear understanding of the intended use of the certificate templates to determine the most suitable remediation approach. This can be achieved through an evaluation of existing certificates and authentication logs, as outlined in the Certified Pre-Owned ADCS whitepaper sections:

  • Monitor User/Machine Certificate Enrollments - DETECT1
  • Monitor Certificate Authentication Events - DETECT2

Collaborate with the individual responsible for AD Certificate Services (ADCS) within the organization to address the following questions pertaining to the identified certificate templates. This process will help in considering the appropriate checks and remedial actions described below:

  1. Is the certificate template in use?
    Check: Latest issued certificates and expiration dates Remediation: Unpublish (Disable) Certificate Template

  2. Which principals are enrolling in this template?
    Check: Requester principals of issued certificates Remediation: Remove "Enroll" Permission (Restrict to Tier Zero)

  3. Does the certificate template require the subjectAlternativeName (SAN) flag?
    Check: If the requester name and the SAN refer to the same principal in issued certificates Remediation: Remove SAN Flag

  4. Could the current setup be replaced with an Enrollment Agent setup?
    Check: If it is feasible that a service account or group of employees in the IT department (potentially non-Tier Zero principals) performs the enrollment on behalf of the users that need the certificate Remediation: Implement Enrollment Agent

  5. Does the certificate template need to allow for authentication?
    Check: Login events using certificates created with the certificate template Remediation: Remove Extended Key Usage (EKU) That Enables Authentication

  6. Could future certificate requests wait for a manual approval?
    Check: If it is feasible that the certificate request has to wait for a Tier Zero principal to manually approve the request
    Remediation: Enable Manager Approval

Unpublish (Disable) Certificate Template

For every Enterprise CA, the certificate template(s) are "PublishedTo":

  1. Log in on the Enterprise CA host with a principal that has the rights to unpublish certificate templates from the Enterprise CA (by default, only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege)

  2. Open "Certification Authority" by searching for the app in the Start menu, or by running certsrv.msc:

  3. Browse to the certificate template, right-click, and click on "Delete" (this will only unpublish the certificate template from the given CA; NOT delete the certificate template):

  4. Confirm you want to disable (unpublish) the certificate template by clicking "Yes" in the prompt

Remove Enroll Permission (Restrict to Tier Zero)

  1. Log in on an Enterprise CA host with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template permissions (by default, only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege)

  2. Open "Certification Authority" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running certsrv.msc:

  3. Right-click on "Certificate Templates" and click on "Manage" to open the Certificate Templates Console:

  4. Right-click on the certificate template for the finding and click on "Properties":

  5. Under "Security", remove the "Full Control" and "Enroll" permissions for the non-Tier Zero principals, and click on "OK":

    Removing "Full Control" remediates "GenericAll" BloodHound edges against the certificate template, and removing "Enroll" remediates "AllExtendedRights" and "Enroll" edges.

Remove SAN Flag

If the certificate template is of schema version 2 or above, then it is possible to disable the SAN flag on the certificate template by selecting "Build from this Active Directory information" under "Subject Name" in the Certificate Templates Console on a CA server:

If the Certificate Template is of Schema Version 1:

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (by default, only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click on "ADSI Edit" and click on "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" Naming Context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click on "Properties":

  6. Scroll down, select "msPKI-Certificate-Name-Flag", and click on "Edit":

  7. If the current number is negative, add 1 to the current number and click "OK"

Example: If the current number is -5, adding 1 to -5 equals -4. If the current number is positive, subtract 1 from the current number and click on "OK". For example, if the current number 5, subtracting 1 from 5 equals 4:

  1. Click on "OK" to apply the changes

Confirm the flag has been removed on a certificate template of schema version 1:

  1. Log in on an Enterprise CA host

  2. Open Certification Authority by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running certsrv.msc:

  3. Right-click on "Certificate Templates" and click on on "Manage" to open the Certificate Templates Console:

  4. Right-click on the certificate template for the finding and click on "Properties":

  5. Confirm that the "Source of subject name" under the "Subject Name" tab is set to "Build from information in Active Directory":

If the Certificate Template is of Schema Version 2 or Higher:

  1. Log in on an Enterprise CA host with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (by default, only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege)

  2. Open Certification Authority by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running certsrv.msc:

  3. Right-click on "Certificate Templates" and click on "Manage" to open the Certificate Templates Console:

  4. Right-click on the certificate template for the finding and click "Properties":

  5. Change the "Subject Name" option to "Build from this Active Directory information" and click on "OK":

Implement Enrollment Agent

The Certificate Request Agent EKU (OID 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.1), known as Enrollment Agent, allows a principal to enroll for a certificate on behalf of another user. An example use case could be a user visiting service desk in-person to obtain a smart card. The service desk personnel then has an Enrollment Agent certificate that allows enrolling a certificate of a smart card certificate template on behalf of that user.

It is also possible to make a service account use an Enrollment Agent certificate, for example, with Windows Hello for Business: Configure and Validate the Public Key Infrastructure in a Hybrid Certificate Trust Model - Windows Security

To create the Enrollment Agent setup, you will need two certificate templates:

  • Enrollment Agent template

    • Has EKU Certificate Request Agent (1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.1)

    • Enrollment Agent user(s) has "Enroll" permission

  • Target template

    • Has the application policy Certificate Request Agent under the issuance requirements

All other settings needs to be configured based on requirements for the specific setup.

Principals with the "Enroll" permission on the Enrollment Agent template will by default be able to enroll on behalf of any user and computer in AD forest including Tier Zero principals and should therefore be treated as Tier Zero. We recommend, however, to implement Enrollment Agent restrictions to only allow the Enrollment Agents to enroll on behalf of the principals that they need to enroll on behalf of and deny all others; most importantly Tier Zero principals. This will allow for the Enrollment Agents to be considered as non-Tier Zero.

The Enrollment Agent restrictions are configured on "Properties" of the CA server in "Certification Authority" on the CA server having the templates published:

Remove EKU That Enables Authentication

We will modify the EKUs directly on certificate template AD object, as it is not possible to edit EKUs of schema version 1 certificate templates in the Certificate Templates Console.

The effective EKUs of a certificate template are stored either in "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" or "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" depending of the schema version (i.e., "msPKI-Template-Schema-Version") of the certificate template. "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" holds the effective EKUs if the schema version is higher that 1 or if "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" is empty (i.e., null). Else, "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" holds the effective EKUs. We recommend to have the same content in both attributes to avoid any mistakes.

  1. Log in on a domain controller (DC) of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (by default, only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu, or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click on "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click on "Properties":

  6. Scroll down, click on "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" and click on "Edit":
    (image: 5-0-6-adsi-mspki-cap.png)

  7. Select and click "Remove" for any EKU matching the following, and click "OK":

    - 2.5.29.37.0 (Any Purpose)

- 1.3.6.1.5.5.7.3.2 (Client Authentication)

- 1.3.6.1.5.2.3.4 (PKINIT Client Authentication)

- 1.3.6.1.4.1.311.20.2.2 (Smart Card Logon)

  8. Repeat the sixth and seventh steps for the "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" attribute and click on "OK" to apply the changes

  9. If there are no EKUs left in "msPKI-Certificate-Application-Policy" or "pKIExtendedKeyUsage" after the removal of the authentication EKUs, then this remediation will become ineffective as no EKUs is interpreted as any EKU; if that is the case, one of the other remediation options must be followed instead

Enable Manager Approval

Manager approval can be activated for certificate templates of any schema version. For certificate templates with schema version 2 or higher, you can enable manager approval by toggling the "CA Certificate Manager Approval" setting within the Certificate Templates Console on the CA server:

The following instructions apply to certificate templates of any schema version.

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (by default, only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click on "ADSI Edit" and click on "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the Certificate Templates container, right-click on the certificate template, and click on "Properties":

  6. Scroll down, click on "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag", and click on "Edit":

  7. Add the number 2 to the existing value and click "OK"

Note: For example, if the current value is 8, then 8 plus 2 is equal to 10:

  1. Click on "OK" to apply the changes

  2. Certificate requests of this certificate template will appear under "Pending Requests" in "Certification Authority" on the CA server from where the requests can be approved or denied:

  3. Consider carefully if the principal in "Requester Name" is allowed to take the identity of the principal specified in the "SAN" attribute of the request before approving the request:
assets.short_description: The principal can perform the AD Certificate Services (ADCS) ESC1 attack against the target domain. This attack allows the principal to impersonate any other principal in the forest.
assets.short_remediation:
Defenders can remediate this finding in several ways, such as unpublishing the affected template(s), restricting enrollment permissions, modifying subject alternate name (SAN) settings, or prohibiting the use of the certificate for authentication.

The finding can also be remediated by revoking permissions on certificate authorities (CAs) or modifying NT authentication trust for the enterprise CA(s). Important: These actions, while effective at reducing risk, carry substantial risk of disrupting legitimate usage of enterprise CA(s).

assets.title: Large Default Groups With GenericWrite Privileges
assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • ATT&CK T1098: Account Manipulation

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • BloodHound Docs: GenericWrite
  • Andy Robbins, Will Schroeder, and Rohan Vazarkar: DerbyCon 2017 - Here be Dragons: The Unexplored Land of Active Directory ACLs

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • Special Identities - Authenticated Users
  • Special Identities - Everyone
  • Active Directory Security Groups - Domain Users
assets.long_description:
The "GenericWrite" privilege grants principals the ability to write to all non-protected properties on the target object. This privilege allows principals to perform abuses against users, groups, and computers such as resetting passwords, adding users to groups, and gaining the ability to perform privileged command execution on computers.

Technical Background

Organizations should arguably never grant large default groups within AD this privilege, as this is against best practice for least privilege. When an adversary discovers an object where most or all domain authenticated users have this privilege, such objects can provide the attacker with their first step in a critical Attack Path leading to the compromise of the entire enterprise.
assets.long_remediation:
Remove large default group control rights on other AD objects.

Removing ACEs From Objects Large Default Groups Control

To manually remove offending access control entries (ACEs):

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open AD Users and Computers (ADUC), click on "View", and enable "Advanced Features"
  2. Locate the object that a large default group controls
  3. Right-click on the object, click on "Properties", open the "Security" tab in the "Properties" window, and click on "Advanced"
  4. Identify the offending ACE, where the "Principal" is a large default group and the "Access" is an abusable privilege

Note: If the "Inherited from" column says "None", proceed to the fifth step. If the "Inherited from" column lists the distinguished name for a parent container, navigate to that container in ADUC and perform the third through fifth steps on the container object.

  1. Click on the offending ACE and click "Remove"
  2. Repeat the fourth and fifth steps for all offending ACEs, click "Ok", and then close the "object properties" window
  3. Repeat these steps for all other affected objects
assets.short_description:
The "GenericWrite" privilege grants principals the ability to write to all non-protected properties on the target object. This privilege allows principals to perform abuses against users, groups, and computers such as resetting passwords, adding users to groups, and gaining the ability to perform privileged command execution on computers.

Organizations should arguably never grant large default groups within AD this privilege, as this is against best practice for least privilege. When an adversary discovers an object where most or all domain authenticated users have this privilege, such objects can provide the attacker with their first step in a critical Attack Path leading to the compromise of the entire enterprise.
assets.short_remediation: Remove the "GenericWrite" privilege that the large default group holds against the AD object.

assets.title: ForceChangePassword Privileges on Objects in Privilege Zone
assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • ATT&CK TA0004: Privilege Escalation
  • ATT&CK T1078: Valid Accounts
  • ATT&CK T1078.002: Domain Accounts

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • BloodHound Docs: ForceChangePassword
  • Windows Ultimate Security: 4724: An Attempt was Made to Reset an Account's Password

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • Protected Accounts and Groups in Active Directory

Other Reference Documentation

  • Trimarc Security: Mitigating Exchange Permission Paths to Domain Admins in Active Directory
assets.long_description:
The "ForceChangePassword" privilege grants principals the ability to set the password of another principal to a known string without knowing the current one.

Only those users belonging to the DOMAIN ADMINS, ENTERPRISE ADMINS, ADMINISTRATORS, and other Tier Zero domain groups should have control over Tier Zero users, groups, computers, and special objects. Users and other principals that do not belong to Tier Zero should not have control over any Tier Zero user, group, computer, or special object.

Technical Background

When a principal has control over another object in AD, that principal could take over the other object in several different ways. For example, control of a user object grants the ability to change that user's password without knowing the current password. Practical attacks exist for a principal having control over several different object types in AD, including the domain object, user objects, computer objects, group objects, and organizational unit (OU) objects.

An attacker with control of a Tier Zero object could abuse that control to impersonate a Tier Zero user, achieve privileged code execution on a Tier Zero computer, or remotely retrieve credential material from domain controllers (DCs).
assets.long_remediation:
Deny all principals in the domain the ability to control a Tier Zero asset except where that principal is itself also a Tier Zero asset.

Removing ACEs From the AdminSDHolder Object

Remove the offending access control entries (ACEs) against each Tier Zero object where the principal referenced in the ACE is not a Tier Zero principal. This process will vary depending on whether the object gets its security descriptor (SD) from the "AdminSDHolder" object. The ProtectAdminGroups background task runs on the PDCe FSMO role holder for the domain every hour, by default. The task first builds a list of protected objects based on the transitive group membership of the highly privileged groups below along with the groups themselves and a few other specific groups and users. Then ProtectAdminGroups compares the SD of the protected object with the SD of the AdminSDHolder object. If they match already ProtectAdminGroups continues on to the next object. If the security descriptors don't match, ProtectAdminGroups stamps the AdminSDHolder SD on the object and sets the adminCount to 1.

BloodHound Enterprise denotes protected objects via the AdminSDHolder Protected property.

AdminSDHolder protected objects include these groups and their transitive membership in the same domain as the AdminSDHolder object:

  • ACCOUNT OPERATORS
  • ADMINISTRATORS
  • BACKUP OPERATORS
  • DOMAIN ADMINS
  • ENTERPRISE ADMINS
  • ENTERPRISE KEY ADMINS (Windows Server 2019+ PDCe)
  • KEY ADMINS (Windows Server 2016+ PDCe)
  • PRINT OPERATORS
  • SCHEMA ADMINS
  • SERVER OPERATORS

Additional protected objects:

  • ADMINISTRATOR (the default RID-500 domain account)
  • DOMAIN CONTROLLERS (group only, no members)
  • KRBTGT
  • READ-ONLY DOMAIN CONTROLLERS (group only, no members)

To remediate the DACL on a protected object, remove the offending ACEs from the "AdminSDHolder" object:

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open AD Users and Computers (ADUC), click on "View", and enable "Advanced Features"

  2. Locate the "AdminSDHolder" object (default location is: "Domain" / "System" / "AdminSDHolder")

  3. Right-click on the "AdminSDHolder" object, click on "Properties", click on the "Security" tab, and click on "Advanced"

  4. Identify the offending ACE where the "Principal" is not a Tier Zero principal and the "Access" is an abusable privilege

  5. Click on the offending ACE and click "Remove"

  6. Repeat the fourth and fifth steps for all offending ACEs, click on "Ok", and close the "AdminSDHolder" properties window

  7. The AdminSDHolder SD changes will be applied to all protected objects in approximately one hour, by default

Removing ACEs From Other Objects

Any user or group that does not get its security descriptor from the "AdminSDHolder" object will need to have offending ACEs removed manually. This will also include all Tier Zero organizational unit (OU) objects.

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open ADUC, click on "View", and enable "Advanced Features"

  2. Locate the Tier Zero object

  3. Right-click on the object, click on "Properties", click on the "Security" tab, and click on "Advanced"

  4. Identify the offending ACE where the "Principal" is not a Tier Zero principal and the "Access" is an abusable privilege

Note: If the "Inherited from" column says "None", proceed to the next step. If the "Inherited from" column lists the distinguished name for a parent container, navigate to that container in ADUC and perform the third, fourth, and fifth steps on the container object.

  1. Click on the offending ACE and click "Remove"

  2. Repeat the fourth and fifth steps for all offending ACEs, click "Ok", and then close the object properties window

  3. Repeat all steps for all other Tier Zero objects

assets.long_description:
The "GenericAll" privilege grants principals the ability to perform nearly any action against the object, including abusable actions such as resetting user passwords and adding new users to security groups. "GenericAll" is also known as "Full Control".

Only those users belonging to the DOMAIN ADMINS, ENTERPRISE ADMINS, ADMINISTRATORS, and other Tier Zero domain groups should have control over Tier Zero users, groups, computers, and special objects. Users and other principals that do not belong to Tier Zero should not have control over any Tier Zero user, group, computer, or special object.

Technical Background

When a principal has control over another object in AD, it is possible for that principal to take over the other object in several different ways. For example, control of a user object grants the ability to change that user's password without knowing the current password. Practical attacks exist for a principal having control over several different object types in AD, including the domain object, user objects, computer objects, group objects, and organizational unit (OU) objects.

An attacker with control of a Tier Zero object could abuse that control to impersonate a Tier Zero user, achieve privileged code execution on a Tier Zero computer, or remotely retrieve credential material from domain controllers (DCs).
assets.long_remediation:
Deny all principals in the domain the ability to control a Tier Zero asset except where that principal is itself also a Tier Zero asset.

Removing ACEs From the AdminSDHolder Object

Remove the offending access control entries (ACEs) against each Tier Zero object where the principal referenced in the ACE is not a Tier Zero principal. This process will vary depending on whether the object gets its security descriptor (SD) from the "AdminSDHolder" object. The ProtectAdminGroups background task runs on the PDCe FSMO role holder for the domain every hour, by default. The task first builds a list of protected objects based on the transitive group membership of the highly privileged groups below along with the groups themselves and a few other specific groups and users. Then ProtectAdminGroups compares the SD of the protected object with the SD of the AdminSDHolder object. If they match already ProtectAdminGroups continues on to the next object. If the security descriptors don't match, ProtectAdminGroups stamps the AdminSDHolder SD on the object and sets the adminCount to 1.

BloodHound Enterprise denotes protected objects via the AdminSDHolder Protected property.

AdminSDHolder protected objects include these groups and their transitive membership in the same domain as the AdminSDHolder object:

  • ACCOUNT OPERATORS
  • ADMINISTRATORS
  • BACKUP OPERATORS
  • DOMAIN ADMINS
  • ENTERPRISE ADMINS
  • ENTERPRISE KEY ADMINS (Windows Server 2019+ PDCe)
  • KEY ADMINS (Windows Server 2016+ PDCe)
  • PRINT OPERATORS
  • SCHEMA ADMINS
  • SERVER OPERATORS

Additional protected objects:

  • ADMINISTRATOR (the default RID-500 domain account)
  • DOMAIN CONTROLLERS (group only, no members)
  • KRBTGT
  • READ-ONLY DOMAIN CONTROLLERS (group only, no members)

To remediate the DACL on a protected object, remove the offending ACEs from the "AdminSDHolder" object:

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open AD Users and Computers (ADUC), click on "View", and enable "Advanced Features"

  2. Locate the "AdminSDHolder" object (default location is: "Domain" / "System" / "AdminSDHolder")

  3. Right-click on the "AdminSDHolder" object, click on "Properties", click on the "Security" tab, and click on "Advanced"

  4. Identify the offending ACE where the "Principal" is not a Tier Zero principal and the "Access" is an abusable privilege

  5. Click on the offending ACE and click "Remove"

  6. Repeat the fourth and fifth steps for all offending ACEs, click on "Ok", and close the "AdminSDHolder" properties window

  7. The AdminSDHolder SD changes will be applied to all protected objects in approximately one hour, by default

Removing ACEs From Other Objects

Any user or group that does not get its security descriptor from the "AdminSDHolder" object will need to have offending ACEs removed manually. This will also include all Tier Zero organizational unit (OU) objects.

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open ADUC, click on "View", and enable "Advanced Features"

  2. Locate the Tier Zero object

  3. Right-click on the object, click on "Properties", click on the "Security" tab, and click on "Advanced"

  4. Identify the offending ACE where the "Principal" is not a Tier Zero principal and the "Access" is an abusable privilege

Note: If the "Inherited from" column says "None", proceed to the next step. If the "Inherited from" column lists the distinguished name for a parent container, navigate to that container in ADUC and perform the third, fourth, and fifth steps on the container object.

  1. Click on the offending ACE and click "Remove"

  2. Repeat the fourth and fifth steps for all offending ACEs, click "Ok", and then close the object properties window

  3. Repeat all steps for all other Tier Zero objects

assets.long_description:
Only Tier Zero principals should be allowed to enroll certificates as other Tier Zero principals in the AD forest.

Technical Background

The principal has control over a victim computer with permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates, configured to enable certificate authentication and require the "dNSHostName" of the enrollee included in the "subjectAlternativeName" (SAN). The victim computer also has enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. AD trusts this enterprise CA for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. There is an affected domain controller (DC) configured to allow "userPrincipalName" UPN certificate mapping. This setup lets the principal impersonate any AD forest computer without their credentials.

The attacker principal can abuse their control over the victim computer to modify the "dNSHostName" attribute of the victim computer to match the "dNSHostName" of a targeted computer. The attacker principal will then abuse their control over the victim computer to obtain the credentials of the victim computer, or a session as the victim computer, and enroll a certificate as the victim in one of the affected certificate templates. The "dNSHostName" of the victim will be included in the issued certificate under its SAN DNS name. The UPN certificate mapping configuration on the affected DCs makes it possible to authenticate over Schannel as the targeted computer. The DC will split the SAN DNS name into a computer name and a domain name, confirm that the domain name is correct, and use the computer name appended with "$" to identify a computer with a matching "sAMAccountName", which the attacker will be authenticated as.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC10b edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC10b edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click on "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

The core of the issue are the affected domain controllers (DCs) which have the weak "userPrincipalName" (UPN) certificate mapping for Schannel authentication enabled. The DCs should be reconfigured to only allow strong certificate mapping to remediate the finding.

The certificate mapping is configured with the registry value "CertificateMappingMethods" under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\Schannel on the DCs. It is bitwise AND value with the possible flags:

  • 0x01 = Subject/Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)
  • 0x02 = Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)
  • 0x04 = UPN certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)
  • 0x08 = S4U2Self certificate mapping (strong)
  • 0x10 = S4U2Self explicit certificate mapping (strong)

Identify DCs With UPN Certificate Mapping Enabled

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound Explore tab to list DCs configured with UPN certificate mapping enabled: MATCH p = (dc:Computer)-[:DCFor]->(:Domain) WHERE dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw >= 0 AND dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw IN [4, 5, 6, 7, 12, 13, 14, 15, 20, 21, 22, 23, 28, 29, 30, 31] RETURN p There may be additional DCs configured with UPN certificate mapping enabled if SharpHound has not performed a successful registry collection on all DCs (requires admin rights). To list all DCs where the Schannel certificate mapping configuration has not been collected, run the below cypher query: MATCH p = (dc:Computer)-[:DCFor]->(:Domain) WHERE dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw IS NULL OR dc.certificatemappingmethodsraw = "-1" RETURN p

Run this PowerShell snippet to check the current value flags enabled the certificate mapping configuration on a given DC: ``` $curValue = (Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\Schannel" -Name "CertificateMappingMethods").CertificateMappingMethods

$flagTable = @{ 0x0001 = "Subject/Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)" 0x0002 = "Issuer certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)" 0x0004 = "UPN certificate mapping (weak – Disabled by default)" 0x0008 = "S4U2Self certificate mapping (strong)" 0x0010 = "S4U2Self explicit certificate mapping (strong)" }

foreach ($flag in $flagTable.Keys) { if ($curValue -band $flag) { "0x$("{0:X4}" -f $flag) $($flagTable[$flag])" } } ```

Identify Certificates Incompatible With Strong Certificate Mapping

A DC will generate an event in the Security log with Windows Event ID (EID) 4624 with the "Logon Process" field set to "Schannel" when the DC authenticates a principal over Schannel. The "Authentication Package" field reveals whether strong or weak certificate mapping was used:

  • Strong certificate mapping = Authentication Package: Kerberos
  • Weak certificate mapping = Authentication Package: Microsoft Unified Security Protocol Provider

Defenders can use the network address and principal names to track down Schannel-based server applications where new certificates must be put in place before strong certificate mapping is enforced, to avoid breaking authentication for the applications.

Ensure the DCs generating these events have strong certificate mapping enabled and not only weak certificate mapping. If strong certificate mapping is not enabled, the DC has no other option than to use weak certificate mapping, even if a certificate complies with strong certificate mapping.

Enforce Strong Certificate Mapping for Schannel Authentication

  1. Log in on the DC as a member of the ADMINISTRATORS group
  2. Open PowerShell as an administrator
  3. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to enforce strong certificate mapping: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\System\CurrentControlSet\Control\SecurityProviders\Schannel" -Name "CertificateMappingMethods" -Value 24

Restart of the DC is not required.

EID 41 in the System|Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log on DCs will reveal failed attempts to authenticate with certificates that do not comply with the strong certificate mapping:

Defenders can use these events to catch whether any system breaks under the enforcement of strong certificate mapping.
assets.short_description: The principal can perform the AD Certificate Services (ADCS) ESC10 Scenario B attack against the target domain. This attack allows the principal to impersonate any computer in the forest.
assets.short_remediation: Disable "userPrincipalName" (UPN) certificate mapping for certificate authentication.

assets.long_description:
Only Tier Zero principals should be allowed to enroll certificates as other Tier Zero principals in the AD forest.

Technical Background

The principal has control over a victim computer with permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates, configured to: 1) enable certificate authentication, 2) require the "dNSHostName" of the enrollee included in the "subjectAlternativeName" (SAN), and 3) do not have the security extension enabled. The victim computer also has enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. There is an affected domain controller (DC) configured to allow weak certificate binding enforcement. This setup lets the principal impersonate any AD forest computer without their credentials.

The attacker principal can abuse their control over the victim computer to modify the victim computer's "dNSHostName" attribute to match the "dNSHostName" of a targeted computer. The attacker principal will then abuse their control over the victim computer to obtain the credentials of the victim computer, or a session as the victim computer, and enroll a certificate as the victim in one of the affected certificate templates. The "dNSHostName" of the victim will be included in the issued certificate under the SAN DNS name. As the certificate template does not have the security extension, the issued certificate will NOT include the security ID (SID) of the victim computer. DCs with strong certificate binding configuration require a SID to be present in a certificate used for Kerberos authentication, but the affected DCs with weak certificate binding configuration will not. The affected DCs will split the SAN DNS name into a computer name and a domain name, confirm that the domain name is correct, and use the computer name appended with a "$" to identify principals with a matching "sAMAccountName". At last, the DC issues a Kerberos ticket-granting ticket (TGT) as the targeted computer to the attacker, which means the attacker now has a session as the targeted computer.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC9b edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC9b edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

The best way of remediating the issue is to perform the follow two steps in the specified order:

  1. Remove the "NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION" flag from certificate templates

  2. Enforce strong certificate binding on all domain controllers (DCs)

We advise gaining a clear understanding of the current usage in the organization of the affected certificate templates with the "NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION" flag to determine if enforcing strong certificate binding could break current workflows and fix those before performing the remediation.

As alternative remediations, you can either harden the affected certificate templates to only allow Tier Zero principals to enroll or enable manager approval. The alternative solutions will likely be temporary, as Microsoft have stated that they will enforce strong certificate binding with a security update in the future (see Reference: KB5014754 - Certificate-Based Authentication Changes on Windows Domain Controllers).

Identify CertTemplates With No Security Extension

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound "Explore" tab to list "CertTemplates" with no security extension:

MATCH (ct:CertTemplate) WHERE ct.nosecurityextension = true RETURN ct

Identify DCs With Weak Certificate Binding Enforcement

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound "Explore" tab to list DCs configured with weak certificate binding:

MATCH (n:Computer) WHERE n.isdc = true AND n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw < 2 RETURN n

There may be additional DCs configured with weak certificate binding if SharpHound has not performed a successful registry collection on all DCs (requires admin rights). To list all DCs where the certificate binding configuration has not been collected, run:

MATCH (n:Computer) WHERE n.isdc = true AND (n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw IS NULL OR n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw = -1) RETURN n

The DC is configured with weak certificate binding enforcement if the the registry value "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement" under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc is set to 0 (Disabled) or 1 (Compatibility Mode).

Identify Certificates Incompatible With Strong Certificate Binding

The Windows May 10, 2022 security update provides audit events that identify certificates that are not compatible with the strong certificate binding. The event IDs (EIDs) are 39-41 under the System\Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log. You should collect and examine those events across DCs to identify any incompatible certificates.

Collaborate with the individuals responsible for AD Certificate Services (ADCS) within the organization to address issues.

Note: The KB article describing the audit events states that the event source is Kdcsvc; however, we have not been able to find the events in this source, but in the "Microsoft-Windows-Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center".

Recommended Remediation: Enforce Strong Certificate Binding

  1. Log in on the DC as a member of the ADMINISTRATORS group

  2. Open PowerShell as an administrator

  3. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to check the current value of the certificate binding enforcement: Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement"
    The value is configured with an integer:
    0: Disabled
    1: Combability mode
    2: Full enforcement mode

  4. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to set strong certificate binding enforcement: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement" -Value 2

  5. Check the value again to verify that the registry value updated: Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement"

Restart of the DC is not required.

EID 41 in the System\Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log on DCs reveals failed attempts to authenticate with certificates not compliant with the strong certificate binding:

Alternative Remediation: Remove No Security Extension Flag

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS in the forest root domain have this privilege by default)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag" and click "Edit":

  7. Extract 524288 from the existing value and click "OK"; for example, if the current value is 524296, then 524296 minus 524288 is equal to 8:

  8. Click "OK" to apply the changes

Alternative Remediation: Enable Manager Approval

While manager approval is enabled on a certificate template, the requested certificate isn't automatically issued by the certificate authority (CA). Instead, issuance occurs only after manual approval by a CA administrator or CA officer.

Manager approval can be activated for certificate templates of any schema version. For certificate templates with schema version 2 or higher, you can enable manager approval specifically by toggling the "CA Certificate Manager Approval" setting within the Certificate Templates Console on the CA server:

The following instructions apply to certificate templates of any schema version.

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS in the forest root domain have this privilege by default)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag" and click "Edit":

  7. Add the number 2 to the existing value and click "OK"

Note: For example, if the current value is 8, then 8 plus 2 is equal to 10:

  1. Click "OK" to apply the changes

  2. Certificate requests of this certificate template will appear under "Pending Requests" in "Certification Authority" on the CA server from where the requests can be approved or denied:  

  3. Consider carefully if the principal in "Requester Name" is allowed to take the identity of the principal specified in the "SubjectAlternateName" (SAN) extension of the request before approving the request:

assets.long_description:
Tightly control domain controllers (DCs) and other computers belonging to Tier Zero to ensure that only users belonging to DOMAIN ADMINS, ENTERPRISE ADMINS, ADMINISTRATORS, and other Tier Zero groups are allowed to execute code on those systems.

Technical Background

DCs store AD privileges and user credential material. An attacker with administrative access on a DC could alter privileges for the purposes of installing persistence mechanisms and could read user credential material from the AD database to impersonate any user in AD. This level of access grants the attacker full control of all identities and assets that AD manages.
assets.long_remediation:
Across all Tier Zero systems, remove any principal that is not Tier Zero from the local groups on each Tier Zero machine that grant privileged and unprivileged code execution privileges.

If Groups Control Group Memberships

  1. Determine which group policy objects (GPOs) are applied to the machine and type the following command:

C:\> gpresult /v /scope computer

  1. Inspect each GPO in GPEdit to determine which policies control local groups; the specific policy is located at: "Computer Configuration" / "Policies" / "Windows Settings" / "Security Settings" / "Restricted Groups"

  2. Right-click on the local group and click on "Properties"

  1. Under "Members of this Group", click on the principal that does not belong to Tier Zero, then click "Remove"; repeat this for all principals that do not belong to Tier Zero

  1. Repeat the third and fourth steps for the following groups, if applicable: ADMINISTRATORS, REMOTE DESKTOP USERS, DISTRIBUTED COM USERS, and REMOTE MANAGEMENT USERS

If Group Memberships Were Set Manually

  1. Log onto the Tier Zero machine as a Tier Zero admin and open a command prompt

  2. Type the following command to list out the members of the local group: net localgroup "administrators"

  3. For each principal that is not part of Tier Zero, type the following command to remove that user from the group (e.g., if the non-Tier Zero principal is a group called DOMAIN USERS):

net localgroup "administrators" "contoso\domain users" /remove

  1. Repeat the second and third steps for the following groups, if applicable: ADMINISTRATORS, REMOTE DESKTOP USERS, DISTRIBUTED COM USERS, and REMOTE MANAGEMENT USERS

Special Considerations for Domain Controllers

When a computer is promoted to a domain controller (DC), members of its local groups are no longer stored in the Security Account Manager (SAM) database but in the AD database (i.e., NTDS.dit). To remove users from the "local" groups on DC, find the following groups in ADUC. Their default locations are:

Contoso.local / Builtin / Administrators
Contoso.local / Builtin / Distributed COM Users
Contoso.local / Builtin / Remote Desktop Users
Contoso.local / Builtin / Remote Management Users
assets.short_description: An attacker with administrative access on a computer can control the target system. Tightly control computers belonging to the Privilege Zone to ensure that only users belonging to the same Privilege Zone are allowed to execute code on those systems.
assets.short_remediation: Remove Principals outside the Privilege Zone from group memberships.

assets.long_description:
Only authorized principals should be granted constrained delegation privileges against Tier Zero computers.

Technical Background

When a principal has the constrained delegation privilege against a computer in AD, that principal may execute privileged code on the computer. Constrained delegation allows the principal to impersonate any other user in AD to any service on the computer where AD trusts it to perform constrained delegation. Users marked "Sensitive and Cannot be Delegated" and members of the PROTECTED USERS security group are not vulnerable to this impersonation.
assets.long_remediation:
Disallow Constrained Kerberos Delegation Against Tier Zero Computers

To remediate the dangerous Kerberos delegation configuration:

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open AD Users and Computers (ADUC)
  2. Click on "View" and enable "Advanced Features"
  3. Locate the computer granted constrained delegation rights against the Tier Zero computer
  4. Right-click the computer and click on "Properties"
  5. In the "Properties" window, click on the "Delegation" tab
  6. In the list below, select "Trust this computer for delegation to specified services only"
  7. Locate and remove the Tier Zero computer
  8. Alternatively, select the radio button marked "Do not trust this computer for delegation"
assets.short_description: Constrained delegation allows one computer to impersonate any other user in the domain against another computer. Only those users that are not marked as "Sensitive and Cannot be Delegated" or added to the PROTECTED USERS group can be abused in this way. Constrained delegation effectively grants administrator rights against the other computer, as it allows for privileged command execution on the other computer.
assets.short_remediation:
• Option A: Remove the computer in the Privilege Zone from the list of computers that trust this computer for constrained delegation
  • Option B: Disallow Kerberos delegation for the computer outside the Privilege Zone

assets.long_description:
The "GenericAll" privilege grants principals the ability to perform nearly any action against the object, including abusable actions such as resetting user passwords and adding new users to security groups. "GenericAll" is also known as "Full Control".

Only those users belonging to the DOMAIN ADMINS, ENTERPRISE ADMINS, ADMINISTRATORS, and other Tier Zero domain groups should have control over Tier Zero users, groups, computers, and special objects. Users and other principals that do not belong to Tier Zero should not have control over any Tier Zero user, group, computer, or special object.

Technical Background

When a principal has control over another object in AD, it is possible for that principal to take over the other object in several different ways. For example, control of a user object grants the ability to change that user's password without knowing the current password. Practical attacks exist for a principal having control over several different object types in AD, including the domain object, user objects, computer objects, group objects, and organizational unit (OU) objects.

An attacker with control of a Tier Zero object could abuse that control to impersonate a Tier Zero user, achieve privileged code execution on a Tier Zero computer, or remotely retrieve credential material from domain controllers (DCs).
assets.long_remediation:
Deny all principals in the domain the ability to control a Tier Zero asset except where that principal is itself also a Tier Zero asset.

Removing ACEs From the AdminSDHolder Object

Remove the offending access control entries (ACEs) against each Tier Zero object where the principal referenced in the ACE is not a Tier Zero principal. This process will vary depending on whether the object gets its security descriptor (SD) from the "AdminSDHolder" object. The ProtectAdminGroups background task runs on the PDCe FSMO role holder for the domain every hour, by default. The task first builds a list of protected objects based on the transitive group membership of the highly privileged groups below along with the groups themselves and a few other specific groups and users. Then ProtectAdminGroups compares the SD of the protected object with the SD of the AdminSDHolder object. If they match already ProtectAdminGroups continues on to the next object. If the security descriptors don't match, ProtectAdminGroups stamps the AdminSDHolder SD on the object and sets the adminCount to 1.

BloodHound Enterprise denotes protected objects via the AdminSDHolder Protected property.

AdminSDHolder protected objects include these groups and their transitive membership in the same domain as the AdminSDHolder object:

  • ACCOUNT OPERATORS
  • ADMINISTRATORS
  • BACKUP OPERATORS
  • DOMAIN ADMINS
  • ENTERPRISE ADMINS
  • ENTERPRISE KEY ADMINS (Windows Server 2019+ PDCe)
  • KEY ADMINS (Windows Server 2016+ PDCe)
  • PRINT OPERATORS
  • SCHEMA ADMINS
  • SERVER OPERATORS

Additional protected objects:

  • ADMINISTRATOR (the default RID-500 domain account)
  • DOMAIN CONTROLLERS (group only, no members)
  • KRBTGT
  • READ-ONLY DOMAIN CONTROLLERS (group only, no members)

To remediate the DACL on a protected object, remove the offending ACEs from the "AdminSDHolder" object:

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open AD Users and Computers (ADUC), click on "View", and enable "Advanced Features"

  2. Locate the "AdminSDHolder" object (default location is: "Domain" / "System" / "AdminSDHolder")

  3. Right-click on the "AdminSDHolder" object, click on "Properties", click on the "Security" tab, and click on "Advanced"

  4. Identify the offending ACE where the "Principal" is not a Tier Zero principal and the "Access" is an abusable privilege

  5. Click on the offending ACE and click "Remove"

  6. Repeat the fourth and fifth steps for all offending ACEs, click on "Ok", and close the "AdminSDHolder" properties window

  7. The AdminSDHolder SD changes will be applied to all protected objects in approximately one hour, by default

Removing ACEs From Other Objects

Any user or group that does not get its security descriptor from the "AdminSDHolder" object will need to have offending ACEs removed manually. This will also include all Tier Zero organizational unit (OU) objects.

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open ADUC, click on "View", and enable "Advanced Features"

  2. Locate the Tier Zero object

  3. Right-click on the object, click on "Properties", click on the "Security" tab, and click on "Advanced"

  4. Identify the offending ACE where the "Principal" is not a Tier Zero principal and the "Access" is an abusable privilege

Note: If the "Inherited from" column says "None", proceed to the next step. If the "Inherited from" column lists the distinguished name for a parent container, navigate to that container in ADUC and perform the third, fourth, and fifth steps on the container object.

  1. Click on the offending ACE and click "Remove"

  2. Repeat the fourth and fifth steps for all offending ACEs, click "Ok", and then close the object properties window

  3. Repeat all steps for all other Tier Zero objects

assets.title: Non-Tier Zero Computer Hosting EnterpriseCA Trusted for NT Authentication
assets.references:

assets.long_description:
Computers hosting an Enterprise certificate authority (CA) should be considered Tier Zero.

Technical Background

The computer hosts the enrollment service of an Enterprise CA, which implies it has the private key of the Enterprise CA's certificate. An attacker could use this private key to sign certificates for authentication as any enabled principal in the AD forest of the domain, as the Enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication and chains up to a root CA.

It may not be possible to obtain the certificate private key if it is protected with a trusted platform module (TPM) or using a hardware security module (HSM). However, it may still be possible to compromise the AD forest. Administrative access to the Enterprise CA host lets an attacker publish certificate templates, approve denied enrollment requests, and more.
assets.long_remediation:
A computer hosting an Enterprise certificate authority (CA) should be considered Tier Zero. SpecterOps, therefore, recommends adding the computer node to Tier Zero rather than attempting to remediate the risk of compromising the computer.

It is possible to prevent the Enterprise CA from being able to issue certificates trusted for NT authentication by removing the EnterpriseCA certificate from the NTAuth store. This will prevent known attacks to compromise the AD forest of the domain; however, we advise against that for several reasons:

  • Legitimate certificates that the Enterprise CA issues could no longer be used for authentication
  • Attackers with administrative access to the host could still create certificates as any principal used for purposes like digital signing; just not authentication
  • The computer object of the Enterprise CA is a member of the CERT PUBLISHERS group, which has considerable privileges like full control over all root CA objects and write access to the userCertificate attributes of all users and computers

There is no known way to abuse the CERT PUBLISHERS group membership to gain control over the domain, but an attacker could break all AD certificate usage in the AD forest, including authentication of Tier Zero principals.
assets.short_description: The computer has a certificate private key that can be abused to sign "golden" certificates for authentication of any enabled principal in the AD forest of the domain.
assets.short_remediation: We advise to add the EnterpriseCA computer to Tier Zero instead of attempting to remediate the risk of having the computer compromised.

assets.title: Logons From Users in Privilege Zone
assets.references:
MITRE ATT&CK

  • ATT&CK TA0006: Credential Access

How Attackers Abuse This Attack Path

  • BloodHound Docs: HasSession

Microsoft Reference Documentation

  • N/A

Other Reference Documentation

  • Windows OS Hub: Workstation Logon Restrictions for AD Users (Log On To)
assets.long_description:
Only use user accounts belonging to the DOMAIN ADMINS, ENTERPRISE ADMINS, ADMINISTRATORS domain groups and other Tier Zero user accounts for tasks that require the higher privileges in AD. Those users should only log onto domain controllers (DCs) or special systems such as privileged access workstations (PAWs).

Technical Background

Whenever a user performs an interactive logon on a system, that system may store the user's password in plaintext in memory. Even with mitigations against plaintext password storage, interactive logons also result in processes running on the system with primary (or "process") tokens for that user. Such tokens can be used to authenticate to other systems without the need to retype a password.

An attacker with administrative access on the system could abuse plaintext password storage or the Windows token model to impersonate the user, perform actions as that user, and abuse whatever privileges that user may have.
assets.long_remediation:
Several mitigations exist which can help with reducing the risk of exposed credentials on systems. This includes just-in-time and just-enough administration, Windows Credential Guard, and Privileged Access Management (PAM) solutions. Those controls may be difficult to apply to the entire enterprise, can be difficult to assess effectiveness, and an attacker may bypass them with admin rights on the system where credentials were exposed.

Instead, prevent exposure of Tier Zero credentials on Tier One and Tier Two systems in the first place, never giving the adversary the opportunity to use those credentials. Several options exist for restricting by technical control where Tier Zero users are allowed to log on, including restricting by Group Policy, using Authentication Silos, or using third-party tools.

Using Group Policy Objects (GPOs) to Restrict Tier Zero Logons

  1. Create a new GPO called "Deny Tier Zero Logon to Lower Tier Systems"

  2. Exclude Tier Zero computers from this policy

    A. Open the server manager dashboard, click "Tools", and click on "Group Policy Management"

B. In the "Group Policy Management" editor, open the GPO you want to apply an exception on (Located in "Group Policy Objects")

C. Click on "Delegation" tab and then "Advanced"

D. Click on "Add", then "Object Types", then "Computers"

E. Search for a Tier Zero computer

F. Choose the computer you entered in step E

G. Locate "Apply group policy" in permissions and checkmark "deny"

H. Click "Apply" and then "OK"

I. Repeat steps C through H for each Tier Zero computer

  3. To verify that Tier Zero computers are filtering out the GPO, run the following command on each Tier Zero computer:

C:\> gpresult /v /scope computer

  1. Apply the logon restriction policies to each Tier Zero user/group

    A. Open "Server Manager Dashboard", click on "Tools", and click on "Group Policy Management"

B. In the "Group Policy Management" editor, right-click on the group policy object you were working with in step 2 and click "Edit"

C. Locate the logon restriction policies in the following locations:
        "Computer Configuration" -> "Policies" -> "Windows Settings" -> "Security Settings" -> "Local Policies" -> "User Rights Assignments" -> "Deny log on as a batch job"

        "Computer Configuration" -> "Policies" -> "Windows Settings" -> "Security Settings" -> "Local Policies" -> "User Rights Assignments" -> "Deny log on as a service"

        "Computer Configuration" -> "Policies" -> "Windows Settings" -> "Security Settings" -> "Local Policies" -> "User Rights Assignments" -> "Deny log on locally"

        "Computer Configuration" -> "Policies" -> "Windows Settings" -> "Security Settings" -> "Local Policies" -> "User Rights Assignments" -> "Deny log on through Remote Desktop Services"

        "Computer Configuration" -> "Policies" -> "Windows Settings" -> "Security Settings" -> "Local Policies" -> "User Rights Assignments" -> "Deny Access to this computer from the network"

  2. For each policy located in step C, right-click on the policy, click "Define these policy settings", click "Add user or group", and add each Tier Zero user or group

  3. Click "Ok" on the "Add User or Group" window, then "Ok" on the "Policy Definition" window
assets.short_description:
Whenever a user performs an interactive logon on a system, that system may store the user's password in plaintext in memory. Even with mitigations against plaintext password storage, interactive logons also result in processes running on the system with primary (or "process") tokens for that user. Such tokens can be used to authenticate to other systems without the need to retype a password.

An attacker with administrative access on the system could abuse plaintext password storage or the Windows token model to impersonate the user, perform actions as that user, and abuse whatever privileges that user may have.
assets.short_remediation: Use group policy objects (GPOs) to restrict logons to computers in the Privilege Zone.

assets.long_description:
Only authorized principals should be granted ownership of other objects in AD.

Technical Background

For example, control of a user object grants the ability to change that user’s password without knowing the current password. Practical attacks exist for a principal having control over several different object types in AD, including the domain object, user objects, computer objects, group objects, and organizational unit (OU) objects.

An attacker with control of a Tier Zero object could abuse that control to impersonate a Tier Zero user, achieve privileged code execution on a Tier Zero computer, or remotely retrieve credential material from domain controllers (DCs).
assets.long_remediation:
Remove non-Tier Zero principal ownership on Tier Zero objects.

Edit the Tier Zero Object Owner Where a Non-Tier Zero Principal is the Current Owner

To edit dangerous group and principal ownerships:

  1. Use a Tier Zero user account on a Tier Zero computer to open AD Users and Computers (ADUC), click "View", and enable "Advanced Features"
  2. Locate the Tier Zero object that a non-Tier Zero principal owns
  3. Right-click on the object, click on "Properties", click on the "Security" tab, and click "Advanced"
  4. At the top of the "Security Descriptor" window, click "Change" next to the currently listed object owner
  5. Search for a new principal to be the object owner (for example, you may change the owner to the DOMAIN ADMINS group)
assets.short_description: Active Directory grants object owners the ability to modify permissions on the object, allowing object owners to introduce an abusable privilege on the object.
assets.short_remediation: Remove Ownership control rights from objects outside the Privilege Zone.

assets.long_description:
The "WriteAccountRestrictions" privilege grants principals the ability to modify several properties on the target principal, most notably the "msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity" attribute. An attacker could exploit the ability to modify the "msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity" property to abuse resource-based constrained delegation (RBCD) to compromise the remote computer system. This property is a binary DACL that controls what security principals can pretend to be any domain user to the particular computer object.

Organizations should only grant those users belonging to the DOMAIN ADMINS, ENTERPRISE ADMINS, ADMINISTRATORS, and other Tier Zero domain groups should have control over Tier Zero users, groups, computers, and special objects. Users and other principals that do not belong to Tier Zero should not have control over any Tier Zero user, group, computer, or special object.

Technical Background

If the "msDS-AllowedToActOnBehalfOfOtherIdentity" discretionary ACL (DAC)L is set to allow an attack-controller account, an attacker could use said account to execute a modified S4U2self/S4U2proxy abuse chain to impersonate any domain user to the target computer system and receive a valid service ticket "as" that user.

assets.long_description:
Only Tier Zero principals should be allowed to enroll certificates as other Tier Zero principals in the AD forest.

Technical Background

The principal has control over a victim principal with permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates, configured to: 1) enable certificate authentication, 2) require the "userPrincipalName" (UPN) of the enrollee included in the "subjectAlternativeName" (SAN), and 3) do not have the security extension enabled. The victim also has enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. There is an affected domain controller (DC) configured to allow weak certificate binding enforcement. This setup lets the principal impersonate any AD forest principal (user or computer) without their credentials.

The attacker principal can abuse their control over the victim principal to modify the victim's UPN to match the "sAMAccountName" of a targeted principal.

Example: If the targeted principal is the ADMINISTRATOR@CORP.LOCAL user, the victim's UPN will be populated with "ADMINISTRATOR" (without the "@CORP.LOCAL" ending).

The attacker principal will then abuse their control over the victim principal to obtain the credentials of the victim principal, or a session as the victim principal, and enroll a certificate as the victim in one of the affected certificate templates. The UPN of the victim (i.e., "Administrator") will appear in the issued certificate under the SAN. As the certificate template does not have the security extension, it will NOT include the security ID (SID) of the victim user in the issued certificate.

Next, the attacker principal will again set the UPN of the victim, this time to an arbitrary string (e.g., the original value). The issued certificate can now be used for authentication against an affected DC. The weak certificate binding configuration on the DC will make the DC accept that the SID of the victim user is not present in the issued certificate when performing Kerberos authentication, and it will use the SAN value to map the certificate to a principal. The DC will attempt to find a principal with a UPN matching the SAN value (i.e., "Administrator") but as the victim's UPN has been changed after the enrollment, there will be no principals with this UPN. The DC will then attempt to find a principal with a "sAMAccountName" that matches the SAN value and find the targeted user.

At last, the DC issues a Kerberos ticket-granting ticket (TGT) as the targeted user to the attacker, which means the attacker now has a session as the targeted user. In case the target is a computer, the DC will find it as well as the DC will attempt to match the "sAMAccountName" with a "$" at the end of the SAN value as a last resort.
assets.long_remediation:
The recommended remediation involves modifying the configuration of the underlying objects and relationships on which the ADCSESC9a edge is based. Explore the composition of an ADCSESC9a edge to view all the underlying objects and relationships:

  1. Go to the "Principals" section at the bottom of this page
  2. Click on the three dots of an instance of the finding and click "Explore"
  3. Click on the edge shown in the graph view to display the entity panel for the edge
  4. Click on "Composition" to see the objects and relationships involved in the finding

The best way of remediating the issue is to perform the follow two steps in the specified order:

  1. Remove the "NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION" flag from certificate templates

  2. Enforce strong certificate binding on all domain controllers (DCs)

We advise gaining a clear understanding of the current usage in the organization of the affected certificate templates with the "NO_SECURITY_EXTENSION" flag to determine if enforcing strong certificate binding could break current workflows, and fix those before performing the remediation.

As alternative remediations, you can either harden the affected certificate templates to only allow Tier Zero principals to enroll or enable manager approval. The alternative solutions will likely be temporary, as Microsoft have stated that they will enforce strong certificate binding with a security update in the future (see Reference: KB5014754 - Certificate-Based Authentication Changes on Windows Domain Controllers).

Identify CertTemplates With no Security Extension

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound "Explore" tab to list "CertTemplates" with no security extension:

MATCH (ct:CertTemplate) WHERE ct.nosecurityextension = true RETURN ct

Identify DCs With Weak Certificate Binding Enforcement

Run the following cypher query in the BloodHound "Explore" tab to list DCs configured with weak certificate binding:

MATCH (n:Computer) WHERE n.isdc = true AND n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw < 2 RETURN n

There may be additional DCs configured with weak certificate binding if SharpHound has not performed a successful registry collection on all DCs (requires admin rights). To list all DCs where the certificate binding configuration has not been collected, run:

MATCH (n:Computer) WHERE n.isdc = true AND (n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw IS NULL OR n.strongcertificatebindingenforcementraw = -1) RETURN n

The DC is configured with weak certificate binding enforcement if the "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement" registry value under HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc is set to 0 (Disabled) or 1 (Combability mode).

Identify Certificates Incompatible With Strong Certificate Binding

The Windows May 10, 2022 security update provides audit events that identify certificates that are not compatible with the strong certificate binding. The event IDs (EIDs) are 39-41 under the System\Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log. You should collect and examine those events across DCs to identify any incompatible certificates.

Collaborate with the individuals responsible for AD Certificate Services (ADCS) within the organization to address issues.

Note: The KB article describing the audit events states that the event source is Kdcsvc; however, we have not been able to find the events in this source, but in the "Microsoft-Windows-Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center".

Recommended Remediation: Enforce Strong Certificate Binding

  1. Log in on the DC as a member of the ADMINISTRATORS group

  2. Open PowerShell as an administrator

  3. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to check the current value of the certificate binding enforcement: Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement"
    The value is configured with an integer:
    0: Disabled
    1: Combability mode
    2: Full enforcement mode

  4. Run this PowerShell cmdlet to set strong certificate binding enforcement: Set-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement" -Value 2

  5. Verify that the registry value was updated by checking the value again: Get-ItemProperty -Path "HKLM:\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\Kdc" -Name "StrongCertificateBindingEnforcement"

Restart of the DC is not required.

Windows EID 41 in the System\Kerberos-Key-Distribution-Center log on DCs will reveal failed attempts to authenticate with certificates not compliant with the strong certificate binding:

Alternative Remediation: Remove No Security Extension Flag

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege by default)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag" and click "Edit":

  7. Extract 524288 from the existing value and click "OK"; for example, if the current value is 524296, then 524296 minus 524288 is equal to 8:

  8. Click "OK" to apply the changes

Alternative Remediation: Enable Manager Approval

While manager approval is enabled on a certificate template, the certificate authority doesn't automatically issue the requested certificate. Instead, issuance occurs only after a CA administrator or CA officer manually approves it.

Manager approval can be activated for certificate templates of any schema version. For certificate templates with schema version 2 or higher, you can enable manager approval specifically by toggling the "CA Certificate Manager Approval" setting within the Certificate Templates Console on the CA server:

The following instructions apply to certificate templates of any schema version.

  1. Log in on a DC of the root domain with a principal that has the rights to modify certificate template attributes (only the ENTERPRISE ADMINS and DOMAIN ADMINS groups in the forest root domain have this privilege by default)

  2. Open "ADSI Edit" by searching for the app in the Start menu or by running adsiedit.msc:

  3. Right-click "ADSI Edit" and click "Connect to…":

  4. Select the "Configuration" naming context and click "OK":

  5. Navigate to the "Certificate Templates" container, right-click on the certificate template, and click "Properties":

  6. Scroll down and select "msPKI-Enrollment-Flag" and click "Edit":

  7. Add the number 2 to the existing value and click "OK"; for example, if the current value is 8, then 8 plus 2 is equal to 10:

  8. Click "OK" to apply the changes

  10. Consider carefully whether the principal in "Requester Name" is allowed to take the identity of the principal specified in the "subjectAlternateName" (SAN) extension of the request before approving the request:

assets.long_description:
Only Tier Zero principals should be allowed to enroll certificates as other Tier Zero principals in the AD forest.

Technical Background

The principal has control over a victim computer with permission to enroll on one or more certificate templates, configured to: 1) enable certificate authentication, 2) require the "dNSHostName" of the enrollee included in the "subjectAlternativeName" (SAN), and 3) do not have the security extension enabled. The victim computer also has enrollment permission for an enterprise certificate authority (CA) with the necessary templates published. This enterprise CA is trusted for NT authentication in the forest and chains up to a root CA for the forest. There is an affected domain controller (DC) configured to allow weak certificate binding enforcement. This setup lets the principal impersonate any AD forest computer without their credentials.

The attacker principal can abuse their control over the victim computer to modify the victim computer's "dNSHostName" attribute to match the "dNSHostName" of a targeted computer. The attacker principal will then abuse their control over the victim computer to obtain the credentials of the victim computer, or a session as the victim computer, and enroll a certificate as the victim in one of the affected certificate templates. The "dNSHostName" of the victim will be included in the issued certificate under the SAN DNS name. As the certificate template does not have the security extension, the issued certificate will NOT include the security ID (SID) of the victim computer. DCs with strong certificate binding configuration require a SID to be present in a certificate used for Kerberos authentication, but the affected DCs with weak certificate binding configuration will not. The affected DCs will split the SAN DNS name into a computer name and a domain name, confirm that the domain name is correct, and use the computer name appended with a "$" to identify principals with a matching "sAMAccountName". At last, the DC issues a Kerberos ticket-granting ticket (TGT) as the targeted computer to the attacker, which means the attacker now has a session as the targeted computer.
